_work.title: Byzantine, Jewish & Eastern-Christian Protective Amulets
_work.creator: various
_work.place: Eastern Mediterranean, the Near East and the Jewish diaspora
_work.rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
_work.blurb: Late-antique, Byzantine, Eastern-Christian and Jewish protective objects — Holy Rider and 'much-suffering eye' amulets, the hystera/womb device against gynaecological demons, Gello/Abyzou and Sisinnios charms, Hebrew childbirth amulets invoking Senoy-Sansenoy-Semangelof against Lilith, gold lamellae, hamsas, the menorah token and silver kameot — spanning the apotropaic traditions of the Greek and Hebrew worlds from antiquity to the early modern era.

entries.title: Byzantine amuletic armband with the Holy Rider (Walters 54.2657)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Byzantine Empire)
entries.rights: Public domain. The Walters Art Museum, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A Byzantine bronze amuletic armband in the Walters Art Museum, encircling the wearer with a band of protective scenes centred on the Holy Rider spearing a fallen demon.

## Iconography
The medallions of the band show the **Holy Rider** — a haloed horseman driving his spear down into a prostrate female figure beneath the hooves — together with ranks of **saints**, sacred scenes, and rows of [[motif:magical-symbols|magical symbols]] (charaktêres). The rider type is captioned on related objects 'Σολομών' (Solomon) or labelled as a warrior saint, and the trampled demon is the child-killing she-demon of the *hystera* tradition.

## Significance
The Holy Rider is the master image of early-Byzantine domestic magic: a fusion of the antique 'Thracian rider', Solomon's legendary mastery over demons, and the warrior saints (Sisinnios, George). Worn as an armband, it placed an unbroken protective circuit around the body, especially for women in childbirth threatened by [[motif:lilith-childbirth|Gylou/Gello]]. The classic studies are Gary Vikan's work on Byzantine amulets and Jeffrey Spier's *Late Antique and Early Christian Gems*.

## For artists & game designers
A wearable 'warding circuit' — a band whose repeating engraved scenes function as a continuous defensive spell wrapped around the arm.

citations.text: Gary Vikan, 'Art, Medicine, and Magic in Early Byzantium,' *Dumbarton Oaks Papers* 38 (1984).
Jeffrey Spier, *Late Antique and Early Christian Gems* (2007).

entries.title: Byzantine amuletic pendant — Holy Rider / 'much-suffering eye' (Walters 54.2653)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Byzantine Empire)
entries.rights: Public domain. The Walters Art Museum, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A Byzantine bronze amuletic pendant in the Walters Art Museum, one of the classic two-sided charms pairing the Holy Rider with the 'much-suffering eye'.

## Iconography
One face bears the **Holy Rider** spearing the demon; the reverse typically shows the **'much-suffering eye'** (the *baskania*) — a single great eye assailed by daggers, arrows, birds, and beasts, with the inscription 'ΦΘΟΝΟϹ' (envy) or an appeal against it. The eye attacked from all sides depicts the [[motif:evil-eye|evil eye]] being turned back upon itself.

## Significance
The rider-and-eye pendant is the single most widespread Byzantine apotropaic type, deployed against the *baskania* (the envious evil eye) believed to cause illness, miscarriage, and the wasting of children. Its imagery descends from the ancient war on the evil eye seen on Roman gems and survives in Greek folk belief to the present. Vikan and Spier remain the standard authorities.

## For artists & game designers
The 'attacked eye' is a perfect anti-curse glyph: a single staring eye pierced by weapons, instantly readable as 'malice returned to sender'.

citations.text: Gary Vikan, 'Art, Medicine, and Magic in Early Byzantium' (1984).

entries.title: Byzantine amuletic pendant, reverse — the 'much-suffering eye' (Walters 54.2653)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Byzantine Empire)
entries.rights: Public domain. The Walters Art Museum, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The reverse of the Walters Byzantine pendant, showing the 'much-suffering eye' in full — the device that gives the amulet its defensive power.

## Iconography
A large frontal **eye** is set upon by **daggers**, lances, birds (an ibis, a raven), a lion, a serpent, and a scorpion, all converging to wound it. A legend names ΦΘΟΝΟϹ (Envy/Phthonos) or invokes the rider-saint. The composition literalizes the destruction of the [[motif:evil-eye|evil eye]] by the very creatures and weapons of the natural and saintly world.

## Significance
The 'much-suffering eye' (*ho polypathês ophthalmos*) is a self-consuming image: the malign eye is shown perpetually attacked so that it can never harm the wearer. It encodes the Byzantine theology of envy as the root of misfortune, countered by saintly and natural assault. The motif passes from these pendants into later Orthodox and Balkan folk amulets. Studied by Vikan and in Maguire's *Byzantine Magic*.

## For artists & game designers
Reverse-side reveals like this teach a clean lesson: an amulet's 'engine' (the eye-trap) hidden on the back, exposed only on inspection.

citations.text: Gary Vikan, *Byzantine Eucharistic Amulets and Magic* (collected studies).

entries.title: Bronze amulet pendant, Byzantine (10th–12th c.)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Byzantine); photograph courtesy CNG
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 3.0 (photo: Classical Numismatic Group). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A middle-Byzantine bronze amulet pendant, representative of the cheap, mass-produced protective jewellery worn across the empire.

## Iconography
The cast **bronze** disc carries a holy figure or cross and a short protective legend, pierced with a loop for suspension at the neck. Such pendants reduce the elaborate rider-and-eye program of earlier amulets to a compact, affordable form for everyday wear.

## Significance
Middle-Byzantine amulet pendants (10th–12th c.) show the continuity of apotropaic practice from late antiquity into the medieval empire. Worn against illness, the [[motif:evil-eye|evil eye]], and demonic attack, they belong to a devotional-magical material culture in which saints' images and holy names did the protective work once carried by [[motif:holy-rider|Holy Rider]] and *hystera* devices. (Dating and details follow the dealer/CNG record.)

## For artists & game designers
The 'common drop' version of a protective charm — a plain cast pendant that signals warding without elaborate iconography.

citations.text: Jeffrey Spier, *Late Antique and Early Christian Gems* (2007).

entries.title: Late-antique amulet (Metropolitan Museum, sf10.130.2540)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Eastern Mediterranean)
entries.date: Late antique / early Byzantine (c. 4th–6th c. CE)
entries.rights: Public domain (CC0). The Metropolitan Museum of Art, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A late-antique amulet in the Metropolitan Museum, illustrating the broad class of small protective objects from the Eastern Mediterranean that bridge the pagan magical gem and the Christian Byzantine charm.

## Iconography
The small amulet bears an engraved protective device — a holy or magical figure with accompanying signs — pierced or framed for wearing. It belongs to the transitional moment when [[motif:amulet|amulets]] carried both the old repertoire of magical figures and the new Christian holy names.

## Significance
Objects like this document the continuity of personal apotropaic magic across the pagan-Christian divide in the late-Roman East. The same anxieties — disease, the [[motif:evil-eye|evil eye]], demonic harm — were addressed first by Greco-Egyptian deities and *voces magicae*, then by saints, crosses, and the [[motif:holy-rider|Holy Rider]]. Spier's catalogue of late-antique gems and amulets situates such pieces in this evolving tradition. (Exact identification follows the Met record.)

## For artists & game designers
A 'transitional artifact' — an object whose iconography straddles two belief-systems, useful for worldbuilding a culture mid-conversion.

citations.text: Jeffrey Spier, *Late Antique and Early Christian Gems* (2007).
Metropolitan Museum of Art, amulet sf10.130.2540.

entries.title: Abyzou — the child-killing demon (line drawing)
entries.creator: Anonymous (after an amulet type)
entries.date: drawing after late-antique / Byzantine amulet prototypes (c. 4th–9th c. CE)
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A line drawing of Abyzou, the child-killing female demon whom the Byzantine Holy Rider amulets were forged to subdue.

## Iconography
**Abyzou** (also Gyllou, Gello, Gylou) is shown as the prostrate she-demon trampled and speared by the [[motif:holy-rider|Holy Rider]], often with serpentine or dishevelled features. On the amulets she lies beneath the horse, named and bound, her depiction being the very means of her defeat.

## Significance
**Abyzou** is the late-antique and Byzantine descendant of the Mesopotamian [[motif:lilith-childbirth|Lamashtu]] and the Jewish Lilith — a demon blamed for miscarriage, infant death, and women's diseases. The *Testament of Solomon* lists her among the spirits Solomon mastered, which is why the rider-amulets invoke Solomon's authority against her. Folklorist studies (A. A. Barb, 'Antaura. The Mermaid and the Devil's Grandmother,' *JWCI* 1966) trace her across cultures. (This is an explanatory drawing after the amulet type.)

## For artists & game designers
The archetypal 'cradle-snatcher' boss — a demon defined entirely by her defeat, perpetually shown bound beneath the hero's spear.

citations.text: A. A. Barb, 'Antaura. The Mermaid and the Devil's Grandmother,' *Journal of the Warburg and Courtauld Institutes* 29 (1966).

entries.title: Sisinnios / Holy Rider amulet (Perdrizet, *Negotium perambulans*, fig. 7)
entries.creator: Drawing published by Paul Perdrizet (1922)
entries.date: engraving after a Byzantine amulet (publ. 1922; prototype c. 6th–10th c. CE)
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
An engraving from Paul Perdrizet's classic study of Byzantine protective magic, reproducing a Holy Rider / Saint Sisinnios amulet that wards the child-snatching demon Gylou.

## Iconography
The rider — here identified with **Saint Sisinnios** — is shown on horseback driving his lance into the **Gylou** demon at his horse's feet. Surrounding inscriptions name the demon's secret names, whose recitation (as in the Sisinnios legend) forces her to surrender the children she has taken.

## Significance
Paul Perdrizet's *Negotium perambulans in tenebris* (1922) was the foundational study of the Byzantine 'much-suffering eye' and Holy Rider amulets and of the legend of **Saint Sisinnios**, who compels the she-demon Gylou/Gello to reveal her twelve-and-a-half names. The amulets transcribe these names because knowing a demon's names binds her — the same logic as the [[motif:bound-demon|incantation bowls]]. The Sisinnios narrative spread from the Greek East into Slavic, Ethiopic, and Arabic traditions.

## For artists & game designers
A 'name-binding' mechanic made visible: defeat the demon not by combat but by inscribing her hidden names around the hero.

citations.text: R. P. H. Greenfield, 'Saint Sisinnios, the Archangel Michael and the Female Demon Gylou,' *Byzantina* 15 (1989).

entries.title: Byzantine protective amulet (Perdrizet, *Negotium perambulans*, fig. 8)
entries.creator: Drawing published by Paul Perdrizet (1922)
entries.date: engraving after a Byzantine amulet (publ. 1922; prototype c. 6th–10th c. CE)
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A second amulet engraving from Perdrizet's *Negotium perambulans*, documenting the combination of rider, eye, and magical signs typical of the Byzantine protective charm.

## Iconography
The drawing reproduces an amulet bearing the [[motif:holy-rider|Holy Rider]] and/or the [[motif:much-suffering-eye|much-suffering eye]], surrounded by **charaktêres** — the looped, ring-tipped magical symbols — and protective inscriptions invoking saints and the power of God.

## Significance
Perdrizet assembled these figures to demonstrate the standardized repertoire of Byzantine apotropaic devices: the rider against the demon, the eye against envy, and the *charaktêres* as a script of pure operative force. His study established how Christian, Jewish, and pagan magical elements coexist on a single object, and remains the point of departure for all later work on Byzantine amulets (Vikan, Spier, Maguire).

## For artists & game designers
A reference sheet of authentic 'magic-script' glyphs — ring-tipped charaktêres that read as a writing system of raw power.

entries.title: Byzantine menorah token (Walters 55.100)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Byzantine Empire)
entries.rights: Public domain. The Walters Art Museum, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A small Byzantine token in the Walters Art Museum stamped with the seven-branched menorah, a portable emblem of Jewish identity and protection in the early-Byzantine world.

## Iconography
The token bears the **menorah**, the seven-branched lampstand of the Jerusalem Temple, often flanked by the **shofar** (ram's horn) and *lulav* (palm branch) — the standard cluster of Jewish symbols in late antiquity. Worn or carried, the menorah image marked the bearer and warded harm.

## Significance
The [[motif:menorah|menorah]] was the pre-eminent symbol of Judaism in the late-antique Mediterranean, appearing on lamps, glass, seals, and amulets. As a protective device it carried the sanctity of the Temple cult into daily life; such tokens attest the lively Jewish communities of the Byzantine Empire and their parallel apotropaic culture to the Christian rider-amulets. The corpus of Jewish symbols is catalogued in E. R. Goodenough's *Jewish Symbols in the Greco-Roman Period*.

## For artists & game designers
A faction-emblem token: a single sacred lampstand that both identifies the bearer's community and confers a protective blessing.

citations.text: E. R. Goodenough, *Jewish Symbols in the Greco-Roman Period* (1953–68).

entries.title: The Lamella Bernensis — inscribed gold amulet
entries.creator: Anonymous (late antique); photograph by Sandstein
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Sandstein). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The *Lamella Bernensis*, a thin inscribed gold sheet of the type rolled into a capsule and worn as a protective phylactery in late antiquity.

## Iconography
The gold **lamella** is covered in finely scratched lines of Greek (or Greek-and-Hebrew) text — divine names, [[motif:voces-magicae|voces magicae]], and protective formulae. Once inscribed, such sheets were tightly **rolled** and sealed inside a tubular capsule hung at the neck.

## Significance
Inscribed gold and silver *lamellae* are the precious-metal cousins of the engraved magical gems, catalogued together as **Greek magical amulets** (Roy Kotansky's corpus). They share the repertoire of Iao, Sabaoth, Adonai, and Abrasax, and the principle that the written name protects the bearer's body and health. The genre is continuous with the Jewish [[motif:gold-lamella|metal amulet]] tradition and the later Byzantine phylactery. (Identification per the Commons file and museum record.)

## For artists & game designers
A 'scroll-in-a-locket' item — readable text rolled away inside a wearable case, the inscription only revealed when unrolled.

entries.title: Ethiopian amulet taken from Emperor Tewodros II
entries.creator: Anonymous (Ethiopian Christian); photograph by Sailko
entries.rights: CC BY 3.0 (photo: Sailko). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
An Ethiopian Christian protective amulet associated with Emperor Tewodros II, taken by a British soldier at Magdala in 1868 and returned in 2003 — a witness to the living Eastern-Christian talismanic tradition.

## Iconography
The amulet is an Ethiopian **talismanic scroll** or pendant inscribed in **Ge'ez** with prayers, the secret names of God and angels, and protective **seals** and stylized eyed figures (*talsam*) designed to repel the evil eye and demons such as the *buda*.

## Significance
Ethiopian (and the related Coptic) Christian magic preserves into modern times the late-antique apotropaic repertoire — divine names, angel names, Solomonic seals, and the rider-saint — that the Byzantine amulets share. Scroll-amulets were prepared by *dabtara* (lay clergy-scribes), cut to the owner's height, against illness and spirit-attack. The provenance — looted at Magdala, repatriated in 2003 — also marks it as an object of colonial restitution. See J. Mercier, *Ethiopian Magic Scrolls* (1979).

## For artists & game designers
A height-measured personal scroll of seals and angel-names is a vivid bespoke-talisman item, tailored literally to its owner's body.

entries.title: Medieval-type Jewish amulet to protect mother and child (Wellcome)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Jewish tradition)
entries.rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A Jewish amulet (*kimiyah*) for the protection of a mother and her newborn, of the type hung in the birth-chamber against the child-killing demon Lilith.

## Iconography
The amulet is inscribed in Hebrew with the names of the three angels **Senoy, Sansenoy and Semangelof**, the formula 'Adam and Eve, out Lilith!' (*ḥuts Lilith*), divine names, [[motif:magic-square|magic squares]], and protective Psalms. It was placed on the walls of the room where mother and infant lay during the dangerous first days.

## Significance
In the legend of the *Alphabet of Ben Sira*, **Lilith** — Adam's rebellious first wife turned night-demon — vows to kill newborns but swears to spare any child protected by the three angels who pursued her. Childbirth [[motif:lilith-childbirth|amulets]] therefore name those angels to bind her oath, the direct Jewish parallel to the Byzantine [[motif:holy-rider|Holy Rider]] against Gylou and the ancient Lamashtu amulets. Joshua Trachtenberg's *Jewish Magic and Superstition* (1939) is the classic study.

## For artists & game designers
A protective ward that works by holding a demon to an oath — its power is contractual, not martial: name the angels and the demon must spare the child.

citations.text: *Alphabet of Ben Sira* (medieval), on Lilith and the three angels.

entries.title: Senoy, Sansenoy and Semangelof — the three protecting angels
entries.creator: Anonymous (after traditional amulet inscriptions)
entries.date: modern rendering of a traditional formula
entries.rights: CC BY 4.0. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
An image of the names of Senoy, Sansenoy and Semangelof, the three angels invoked on every Jewish childbirth amulet against Lilith.

## Iconography
The three **angel names** are written in Hebrew, the core text of the *kimiyah* hung over a crib or birth-bed. On full amulets they accompany the figures of Adam and Eve and the command expelling [[motif:lilith|Lilith]].

## Significance
When Lilith fled Eden and the sea, the three angels **Senoy, Sansenoy and Semangelof** pursued her; she swore that wherever she saw their names she would not harm a mother or child (*Alphabet of Ben Sira*). Their names are thus the operative formula of Jewish [[motif:lilith-childbirth|childbirth magic]], reproduced on amulets, *Shir ha-Ma'alot* sheets, and birth-room decorations down to the present. Trachtenberg and Raphael Patai (*The Hebrew Goddess*) trace the tradition.

## For artists & game designers
Three named guardian-spirits whose mere inscription enforces a demon's vow — a tidy 'invoke-by-name' protective spell.

citations.text: Raphael Patai, *The Hebrew Goddess* (1967), ch. on Lilith.

entries.title: Amulet-tableau protecting women in childbirth (Musée alsacien, Strasbourg)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Alsatian Jewish); photograph by Ji-Elle
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Ji-Elle). Musée alsacien de Strasbourg, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
An Alsatian Jewish childbirth amulet-tableau (a *Kimpetbrief*) from the Musée alsacien, hung in the lying-in room to guard mother and child.

## Iconography
The printed or written **tableau** carries Hebrew protective texts — Psalm 121 ('I lift my eyes to the hills'), the names **Senoy, Sansenoy and Semangelof**, divine names, and the expulsion of [[motif:lilith|Lilith]] — arranged around figures of Adam and Eve and protective formulae, framed for hanging on the chamber wall.

## Significance
The Ashkenazi *Kimpetbrief* ('childbed letter') is the framed, wall-hung form of the Jewish [[motif:lilith-childbirth|childbirth amulet]], standard in 18th–19th-century European Jewish homes. It gathers the angelic names, Psalms, and the legend of Lilith and the three angels into a single protective placard for the most perilous moment of a woman's life. Such objects are studied in the literature on Jewish folk and ritual art and on the Lilith tradition.

## For artists & game designers
A framed 'room ward' — a printed sheet that passively protects everyone in the chamber where it hangs.

entries.title: Childbirth amulet by the scribe David Elias Krieger, Galicia (c. 1900)
entries.creator: David Elias Krieger (scribe)
entries.rights: Public domain (CC0). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A finely lettered childbirth amulet for mother and newborn, written by the trained scribe David Elias Krieger in Galicia around 1900.

## Iconography
The parchment **kimiyah** is densely inscribed in scribal Hebrew with the names of God, the angels **Senoy, Sansenoy and Semangelof**, protective verses, and the formula barring [[motif:lilith|Lilith]] from the mother and her infant — the work of a *sofer* trained in the exacting calligraphy required for holy texts.

## Significance
That the amulet is signed by a named, trained scribe underlines the seriousness of [[motif:lilith-childbirth|childbirth protection]] in Eastern European Jewish life around 1900: the efficacy of the *kimiyah* depended on correct, sanctified lettering of the divine and angelic names. Such late examples show the unbroken continuity of the Lilith-and-three-angels tradition from the medieval *Alphabet of Ben Sira* into the modern era. Trachtenberg documents the practice.

## For artists & game designers
A reminder that in this tradition the *quality of the inscription* is the magic — a craftable item whose power scales with the scribe's skill.

citations.text: *Alphabet of Ben Sira* (medieval), on Lilith and the three angels.

entries.title: Kabbalistic amulet for the cure of jaundice (Wellcome L0038188)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Jewish tradition)
entries.rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
An 18th-century Kabbalistic amulet inscribed specifically for the cure of jaundice, in the Wellcome Collection — a medical *segulah* of practical Kabbalah.

## Iconography
The amulet pairs Hebrew **divine names** and angel names with a **magic square** or letter-grid and a directive naming the ailment. As with all *segulot*, the manipulation of holy names is held to act directly on the body, here drawing off the yellow sickness.

## Significance
Healing amulets keyed to a single named disease show the everyday, therapeutic face of practical Kabbalah (*kabbalah ma'asit*). Jaundice (*yerakon*) was a common target of such charms across Jewish communities, the amulet functioning as a prescription written in sacred names rather than in herbs. The tradition is surveyed by Trachtenberg and, for the ancient roots, by Gideon Bohak's *Ancient Jewish Magic*.

## For artists & game designers
A disease-specific cure item — a charm whose inscription literally names the malady it lifts, a clean model for targeted healing consumables.

citations.text: Gideon Bohak, *Ancient Jewish Magic: A History* (2008).

entries.title: Jewish manuscript amulet (Wellcome Collection)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Jewish tradition); Wellcome Collection
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A handwritten Jewish manuscript amulet (*kimiyah*) from the Wellcome Collection, combining holy names, seals, and letter-figures for protection.

## Iconography
The parchment leaf carries lines of Hebrew **divine and angel names**, [[motif:magic-square|magic squares]], looped seals, and protective verses, laid out so the holy words form a defensive grid. Such a sheet was rolled or folded into a case and worn or hung at home.

## Significance
Manuscript amulets are the everyday medium of practical Kabbalah, written to order by a *ba'al shem* or scribe against a specific danger — illness, the [[motif:evil-eye|evil eye]], demonic harm, or the perils of childbirth. They draw on the same name-magic codified in the [[motif:practical-kabbalah|Sefer Raziel]] and on far older Aramaic and Greek amulet traditions. Bohak and Trachtenberg are the standard guides. (Details per the Wellcome record.)

## For artists & game designers
A hand-inscribed charm laid out as a grid of holy names — the 'spell as document' that a scribe-class character produces on demand.

citations.text: Gideon Bohak, *Ancient Jewish Magic: A History* (2008).

entries.title: Hebrew Kabbalistic charms
entries.creator: Anonymous (Jewish tradition); photograph by ranshoket
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 2.0 (photo: ranshoket). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A group of Hebrew Kabbalistic charms — small worn amulets inscribed with divine names — representing the popular practical-Kabbalah amulet still in use today.

## Iconography
The **charms** are small metal or parchment [[motif:kimiyah|kimiyot]] bearing Hebrew **divine names**, abbreviations, and protective formulae, often hung on a chain or pinned to clothing. They condense the longer manuscript amulets into compact, wearable tokens.

## Significance
The continuous use of such charms from antiquity to the present shows the resilience of Jewish [[motif:name-magic|name-magic]]. The names of God and the angels, the *Shem ha-Mephorash*, and protective Psalms are believed to guard against the [[motif:evil-eye|evil eye]], illness, and misfortune. The tradition is rooted in the *Sefer Raziel* and earlier Geniza amulets, surveyed by Bohak and Trachtenberg.

## For artists & game designers
A tray of small interchangeable charms — each a single-effect token the player can mix and match on a chain.

citations.text: Gideon Bohak, *Ancient Jewish Magic: A History* (2008).

entries.title: Jewish amulet (Hungarian Jewish Museum, Budapest)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Jewish tradition); photograph by Szilas
entries.rights: Public domain. Hungarian Jewish Museum, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A Jewish amulet in the Hungarian Jewish Museum, an ornamented silver plaque of the kind worn or hung for protection in Central European Jewish communities.

## Iconography
The shaped metal **amulet** carries Hebrew **divine names** and protective formulae, frequently framed by a **crown**, columns, or a small [[motif:menorah|menorah]], with a loop for suspension. The decorative casing dignifies the holy text it bears.

## Significance
Silver amulet-plaques were treasured objects in Ashkenazi and Central European Jewish homes, combining fine metalwork with the protective name-magic of practical Kabbalah. Hung in the home or worn at moments of danger, they guard against the [[motif:evil-eye|evil eye]], illness, and harm to mother and child. Such pieces are catalogued in the literature on Jewish ceremonial silver and folk magic. (Details per the museum record.)

## For artists & game designers
The 'fine-craft' tier of a charm — the same protective text upgraded into worked silver with crown and columns, signalling a rarer, costlier ward.

entries.title: Silver amulet with hanging pendants (Yemenite/Near-Eastern Jewish)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Near-Eastern Jewish); photograph by Davidbena
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Davidbena). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A Near-Eastern (Yemenite-style) Jewish silver amulet hung with small dangling pendants, blending fine filigree craft with protective name-magic.

## Iconography
The central **silver** case or plaque, worked in **filigree** and granulation, holds an inscribed [[motif:kimiyah|kimiyah]] and is fringed with small **hanging pendants** and bells. Movement and sound, as well as the inscribed names, were felt to drive off harm and the [[motif:evil-eye|evil eye]].

## Significance
Yemenite and other Near-Eastern Jewish silversmiths produced some of the most elaborate amulet-jewellery in the Jewish world, uniting the kabbalistic name-magic of the *kimiyah* with a regional aesthetic shared with their Muslim neighbours (the dangling pendants and bells recall the wider Near-Eastern protective-jewellery tradition, including the [[motif:hamsa|hamsa]]). Such pieces are studied in works on Jewish ethnographic art (e.g. the Israel Museum collections).

## For artists & game designers
A charm that protects by motion and chime as well as inscription — jangling pendants whose sound itself is part of the ward.

citations.text: Esther Juhasz (ed.), *Sephardi Jews in the Ottoman Empire* (Israel Museum, 1990).

entries.title: Silver Hand of Fatima / hamsa (Morocco)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Moroccan); photograph by Andreas Praefcke
entries.rights: Public domain (photo: Andreas Praefcke). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A Moroccan silver hamsa — the open right hand (Hand of Fatima / Hand of Miriam) — the great shared apotropaic emblem of Jews and Muslims across North Africa and the Near East.

## Iconography
The **hamsa** (Arabic *khamsa*, 'five') is a stylized open **hand**, often symmetrical with the thumbs forming a second pair of fingers, frequently set with an eye at the palm. The five fingers and the central eye together repel the [[motif:evil-eye|evil eye]].

## Significance
The [[motif:hamsa|hamsa]] is among the oldest and most widespread protective signs of the Mediterranean and Near East, embraced as the **Hand of Miriam** in Jewish tradition and the **Hand of Fatima** in Islam. Its 'five' wards off the envious gaze (Hebrew *ḥamesh*, the number five, itself thought protective). The motif descends from ancient hand-amulets and remains ubiquitous in Jewish and Muslim folk culture. See the studies on the evil eye by Alan Dundes (ed.), *The Evil Eye: A Casebook* (1981).

## For artists & game designers
The single most recognizable anti-evil-eye sigil — an open hand with an eye in the palm, instantly legible across cultures.

citations.text: Alan Dundes (ed.), *The Evil Eye: A Casebook* (1981).

entries.title: Jewish necklace with amulets to ward off evil, Los Roquetes (c. 1350)
entries.creator: Anonymous (medieval Catalan Jewish); photograph by Joe Mabel
entries.rights: Public domain (photo: Joe Mabel). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A medieval Jewish necklace strung with protective amulets from Los Roquetes, Catalonia (c. 1350), a rare dated survival of Sephardi apotropaic jewellery.

## Iconography
The **necklace** combines beads with small **amulet pendants** — pierced charms, possibly inscribed or set with protective stones — assembled into a single warding ornament worn at the throat against the [[motif:evil-eye|evil eye]] and harm.

## Significance
Dated around 1350, on the eve of the catastrophes that would end Iberian Jewry, the necklace documents the everyday protective jewellery of medieval Sephardi communities. Stringing several amulets together multiplied their power, a practice paralleled across Mediterranean cultures. As an archaeological find it anchors the otherwise hard-to-date tradition of Jewish [[motif:amulet|amulet]]-wearing in a precise medieval context.

## For artists & game designers
A 'stacked charm' necklace — several small amulets combined into one wearable, modelling an inventory where protective effects accumulate.

citations.text: Vivian B. Mann (ed.), *Uneasy Communion: Jews, Christians and the Altarpieces of Medieval Spain* (2010).

entries.title: Talismanic medallion with a magic square (Portable Antiquities Scheme)
entries.creator: Anonymous; recorded by the Portable Antiquities Scheme (photo: Alex Whitlock)
entries.rights: CC BY 2.0 (Portable Antiquities Scheme). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A post-medieval copper-alloy talismanic pendant recorded by the Portable Antiquities Scheme, carrying a magic square — the numerical amulet shared by the Hebrew, Islamic, and Latin magical traditions.

## Iconography
The **medallion** bears a **magic square**: a grid of numbers (or letter-numerals) in which every row, column, and diagonal sums to the same total. Such squares were assigned to planets and powers and engraved on pendants to draw their influence and protect the wearer.

## Significance
The planetary [[motif:magic-square|magic squares]] (the *kameot* of Hebrew tradition, the *wafq* of Arabic, the *tabula* of Latin grimoires) are a truly cross-cultural amulet form, codified for the seven planets in works like Agrippa's *De occulta philosophia* and rooted in earlier Arabic and Hebrew sources. As a worn talisman the square binds the abstract harmony of number to the body. Studied in the scholarship on planetary talismans and the *Picatrix* tradition. (Dating and find-data per the PAS record.)

## For artists & game designers
A pure-number amulet — a grid whose balanced sums *are* the magic, ideal for a puzzle-locked talisman the player must complete.

citations.text: Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa, *De occulta philosophia* (1533), bk II on planetary squares.

entries.title: The Magic Square of Ithiel
entries.creator: Elijah N. James (photograph / rendering)
entries.date: modern rendering of a traditional magic-square amulet
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 3.0 (Elijah N. James). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A 'Magic Square of Ithiel', a Hebrew letter-square amulet of the kind used in practical Kabbalah, where a holy name is woven into a symmetrical grid.

## Iconography
The **square** arranges Hebrew **letters** — spelling and permuting a divine or angelic name — into a grid readable in several directions, so that the Name is repeated and folded upon itself. The geometric closure of the figure 'seals' its power.

## Significance
Letter- and name-squares (a sub-type of the *kameah*) belong to the same family as the numerical [[motif:magic-square|magic squares]], but here the operative content is a sacred **name** rather than numbers. Permuting and arraying the divine name (*tzeruf*) is a core technique of practical Kabbalah, held to concentrate the Name's protective force. Such squares appear in the *Sefer Raziel* and related handbooks. (The specific 'Ithiel' attribution follows the Commons file; the form is traditional.)

## For artists & game designers
A word-search-like sigil where a hidden holy name, read in many directions, powers the charm — a built-in puzzle whose solution is the spell.

citations.text: Moshe Idel, *Kabbalah: New Perspectives* (1988), on letter-permutation (tzeruf).
